message:
fix(fcp-datadog): split monitor.delete behind dedicated capability (resolves br-m7s4q)

Resolves flywheel_connectors-m7s4q — [REVIEW-A9][P2] connector
authorization: `datadog.monitors.delete` was gated by the generic
`datadog.monitors.write` capability shared with monitor
creation/update.

**Previous behavior (over-broad capability):**

`connectors/datadog/src/connector.rs:931` declared the
`monitors.delete` OperationInfo with `capability =
"datadog.monitors.write"`, the same string used for
`monitors.create`. A token issued to a workflow that legitimately
needs to define or update monitor definitions could ALSO destroy
them — even though `monitors.delete` is `RiskLevel::High` /
`SafetyTier::Dangerous` / irreversible, while
`monitors.create`/`update` are `RiskLevel::Medium` /
`SafetyTier::Risky` and reversible.

This violated the principle of least authority for destructive
operations. Datadog's own API treats deletion as a separately
permissioned action.

**Fix:**

- `monitors.delete` operation now requires capability id
  `"datadog.monitors.delete"` (line 946). Holders that
  legitimately need delete semantics must request the dedicated
  capability explicitly; tokens that only carry
  `datadog.monitors.write` cannot invoke delete.
- Handshake response (line 295) advertises the new capability so
  callers can request it during preflight / token issuance.
- Inline comments at both sites name the bead and explain the
  least-authority split.
- `monitors.create` and `monitors.update` keep the generic
  `monitors.write` capability — read/create/update workflows are
  unchanged.

**Regression tests (1 new + 1 augmented):**

- `operations_monitor_delete_has_dedicated_capability`: locks in
  that `monitors.delete.capability == "datadog.monitors.delete"`,
  `monitors.create.capability == "datadog.monitors.write"`, and
  the two are NOT the same string. A future regression that
  re-conflates them fails at test time.
- `lifecycle_handshake_capabilities` (integration test) augmented
  to assert the handshake response now advertises the
  `datadog.monitors.delete` capability alongside the existing
  ones. Without this assertion, the handshake could silently
  drop the new capability and callers wouldn't be able to
  request it.

273 fcp-datadog tests passing (227 lib + 2 doctests + 44
integration; +1 lib regression vs. pre-fix).

**Operator action:**

Existing tokens that named only `datadog.monitors.write` will no
longer authorize monitor deletion — desired behavior; that was the
bug. Workflows that actually need to delete monitors must update
their token issuance to request `datadog.monitors.delete`
explicitly.

files.patch:
@@ -286,6 +286,13 @@ impl DatadogConnector {
                 "datadog.metrics.write",
                 "datadog.monitors.read",
                 "datadog.monitors.write",
+                // br-m7s4q: monitor deletion is irreversible
+                // (RiskLevel::High / SafetyTier::Dangerous) and is gated
+                // by a dedicated capability so a token issued for
+                // creating/updating monitor definitions cannot also
+                // destroy them. Holders that legitimately need delete
+                // semantics must request both.
+                "datadog.monitors.delete",
                 "datadog.logs.read"
             ]
         }))
@@ -928,7 +935,15 @@ fn operations_info() -> Vec<OperationInfo> {
                 }
             }),
             json!({ "type": "object" }),
-            "datadog.monitors.write",
+            // br-m7s4q: split destructive monitor.delete behind its own
+            // capability id. Pre-fix used the generic
+            // `datadog.monitors.write` (the same id as monitors.create
+            // and monitors.update), so a token issued to a workflow
+            // that needed to define/update monitors could ALSO delete
+            // them — RiskLevel::High + SafetyTier::Dangerous +
+            // irreversible action. Holders that legitimately need to
+            // delete monitors must now request the dedicated capability.
+            "datadog.monitors.delete",
             RiskLevel::High,
             SafetyTier::Dangerous,
             IdempotencyClass::Strict,
@@ -1474,6 +1489,46 @@ mod tests {
         assert!(ids.contains(&"datadog.monitors.list"));
     }
 
+    /// br-m7s4q: monitor.delete must require a DEDICATED capability,
+    /// not the generic monitors.write shared with create/update.
+    /// Lock in the split so a future regression that re-conflates
+    /// them is caught at test time.
+    #[test]
+    fn operations_monitor_delete_has_dedicated_capability() {
+        let ops = ops_json();
+        let arr = ops.as_array().unwrap();
+
+        let delete = arr
+            .iter()
+            .find(|op| op["id"] == "datadog.monitors.delete")
+            .expect("monitor.delete operation must be present");
+        assert_eq!(
+            delete["capability"].as_str().unwrap(),
+            "datadog.monitors.delete",
+            "delete must require its own capability id, not the generic write",
+        );
+        // Sanity: risk + safety tier remain dangerous.
+        assert_eq!(delete["risk_level"], "high");
+        assert_eq!(delete["safety_tier"], "dangerous");
+
+        // create/update style operation should NOT route through
+        // the new delete capability.
+        let create = arr
+            .iter()
+            .find(|op| op["id"] == "datadog.monitors.create")
+            .expect("monitor.create operation must be present");
+        assert_eq!(
+            create["capability"].as_str().unwrap(),
+            "datadog.monitors.write",
+            "create still uses the generic write capability",
+        );
+        assert_ne!(
+            create["capability"].as_str().unwrap(),
+            delete["capability"].as_str().unwrap(),
+            "create and delete must require different capabilities",
+        );
+    }
+
     #[test]
     fn operations_all_have_idempotency() {
         for op in ops_json().as_array().unwrap() {
